items.passage: Soap works because each molecule has a special structure that connects two chemicals which normally repel. One end of the molecule is attracted to water, while the other end clings tightly to oily grease. When soap surrounds a drop of grease, it forms a tiny cluster that lifts the dirt away. The trapped grease is then carried off easily as flowing water rinses across the surface. Because these clusters keep oil suspended, soap can generally clean fabrics and skin that water cannot.

items.passage: A glass prism can bend a beam of ordinary sunlight and separate it into many distinct colors. Light slows down as it enters the denser glass, and this change forces each ray to bend. Every color travels at a slightly different speed, so the colors spread apart inside the prism. The familiar band of colors that appears is often called the visible spectrum. Although the effect seems simple, it reveals how ordinary white light is actually a mixture of colors.

items.passage: Hydroelectric dams generate electricity by using the powerful movement of water stored behind a massive concrete wall. As the water falls, it rushes through wide pipes and spins large machines called turbines. The spinning turbines drive generators, which convert the motion into a steady flow of electric current. While engineers can control the water's release, the supply of power is adjusted to meet changing demand. Because rivers are renewed by rainfall, this method generally produces clean energy without burning any fuel.

items.passage: The first coins appeared thousands of years ago when busy merchants needed a reliable way to measure value. Before coins existed, people traded goods directly, but matching what each person wanted was often quite difficult. Early coins were made from precious metals and stamped with official marks that guaranteed their weight. Because the stamp promised a fixed amount of metal, buyers could trust the value of each piece. This trusted system spread quickly, and coined money gradually replaced the clumsy exchange of raw goods.

items.passage: A mosaic is a large picture built from thousands of small colored pieces set into wet plaster. Ancient workers cut the cubes from stone, glass, and shell to form these lasting designs. Each piece was placed by hand, and the narrow gaps between them were filled with cement. When light strikes the rough surface, the many colors seem to shimmer as viewers slowly move. Although the work was slow, a mosaic could survive for centuries and often covered floors and walls.

items.passage: Fog is essentially a cloud that develops near the ground whenever moist air cools below a critical temperature. As the air cools, it can no longer hold all of its invisible water vapor. The extra vapor is squeezed into countless tiny droplets that float and scatter the surrounding light. These droplets hang in the still air, and they reduce how far a person can clearly see. Fog usually forms on calm, clear nights, but it typically fades once the morning sun warms the land.
